symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden severe cramping calf pain at the peak of prolonged exertion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Instantaneous onset at peak exertion is the defining temporal signature of an exertional cramp.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: DVT pain characteristically builds gradually over hours to days, not instantaneously at peak exertion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Brief relief on passive stretch with recurrence at rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Stretch-responsiveness reflects the Golgi-tendon-organ reflex mechanism specific to cramp; neither DVT nor a structural tear behaves this way.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Thrombotic pain is not relieved by stretching the muscle.

symptoms.symptom_name: Discrete visible/palpable hard knot in the muscle belly (not diffuse leg swelling)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A focal, palpable contracted muscle belly is the physical hallmark of an active cramp.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: DVT produces diffuse calf swelling, warmth and erythema rather than a discrete hard knot.

symptoms.symptom_name: Heavy sweating with plain-water rehydration during a prolonged run in the heat

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dehydration and sodium depletion from heavy sweating replaced with plain water is a recognised contributor to exertional cramps.

symptoms.symptom_name: No prior venous thromboembolism, no recent immobility or long-haul travel, active runner

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of VTE risk factors keeps the Wells score low and the pre-test probability of DVT low.

symptoms.symptom_name: No chest pain, breathlessness, or haemoptysis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of cardiorespiratory symptoms lowers concern for PE as a complication of an unrecognised DVT.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intact toe movement and sensation, no numbness/paraesthesia, foot not cold

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved sensation and motor function reduce (but cannot remotely exclude) compartment syndrome and arterial ischaemia.
A warm foot with intact sensation argues against acute arterial occlusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Distal pulses bounding and equal bilaterally; capillary refill <2 seconds

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal distal perfusion excludes arterial ischaemia — a clinician-only finding sealed from the AI Doctor, who must instead infer perfusion from patient-reportable proxies (foot colour, warmth, sensation).

symptoms.symptom_name: Focal rock-hard tender spasm in the medial gastrocnemius; surrounding tissue not uniformly oedematous; no palpable muscle defect

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Palpation of a discrete contracted muscle without diffuse oedema or a defect confirms cramp over DVT or major tear — a clinician-only finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured vitals: BP 142/88, HR 98, Temp 37.1, RR 18, SpO2 99%

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mild tachycardia and blood-pressure elevation are consistent with acute pain and recent exertion, not a specific diagnostic pointer; clinician-measured and sealed.
Normal electrolytes and CK help confirm the diagnosis in recurrent/severe cases but are not acutely indicated and are unavailable in telehealth.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Together — instantaneous exertional onset, stretch-responsiveness, a focal muscle knot, and dehydration/electrolyte context — these strongly identify a benign cramp and discriminate it from DVT and structural injury.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Clinical DVT risk stratification (Wells score) from history
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: A telehealth-computable risk tool; a low Wells score with a classic exertional cramp supports conservative management without imaging.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: D-dimer / compression ultrasound of the calf
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated acutely given a classic exertional cramp with low pre-test probability; over-ordering drives a false-positive cascade. Reserve for cases where the history or evolving red flags raise DVT probability.
Not acutely indicated (per the source clinician); useful only for recurrent or severe cramping, or if rhabdomyolysis is suspected (dark urine).

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Urine dipstick for blood/myoglobin (if urine turns dark)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Conditional — a screen for rhabdomyolysis if the patient later reports tea/cola-coloured urine.
